"And Israel took his journey with all that he had, and came to Beersheba, and offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac." On "came to Beersheba," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the place. Calvin and Gill note Beersheba — the place sacred to the patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac); the border of the promised land (Jacob pausing before leaving it); the place where God had appeared to Isaac (26:24); the fitting place for Jacob to seek God.

On "offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the worship: "offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac" — Jacob worshipped (offered sacrifices) before leaving Canaan — to seek God's guidance and blessing on so great a step (going down to Egypt); the piety of the patriarch (not going without God); the God "of his father Isaac" (the covenant God); the worship that drew the vision (vv.2-4). The verse displays Jacob journeying to Beersheba and offering sacrifice.

"And he said, I am God, the God of thy father: fear not to go down into Egypt; for I will there make of thee a great nation:" The witnesses note God's promise reassuring Jacob's descent into Egypt — the crux of the covenant providence. On "I am God, the God of thy father," the grammatical voices note the assurance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I am God, the God of thy father" — God identified himself as the covenant God of Isaac (and Abraham); the assurance of the covenant; the God who had promised the land (28:13-15) now sanctioning the descent into Egypt; the ground of Jacob's confidence.

On "fear not to go down into Egypt," interpreters expound the reassurance. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note God's word — "fear not to go down into Egypt": God reassured Jacob against his fears of going down to Egypt (the land of his fathers' sojourning fears, where Abraham had gone in famine, 12:10, and Isaac had been forbidden to go, 26:2); Jacob might fear to leave the promised land, or that the descent meant the loss of the covenant hope, or the dangers of Egypt; God bade him "fear not" — the descent was of God's ordering (the famine, Joseph's exaltation, the preservation of the family, 45:5-8); the divine sanction of the going-down; the providence over the whole Egyptian sojourn (foretold to Abraham, 15:13-16) now begun under God's hand.

On "I will there make of thee a great nation," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the promise: "I will there make of thee a great nation" — the great promise (the seventy souls, v.27, would become a great nation in Egypt, Exodus 1:7); the covenant promise (12:2; 15:5) to be fulfilled in Egypt (the nation born there, brought out at the Exodus); the providence — Egypt the womb of the nation; the promise that turned the descent into the means of the covenant's fulfillment. The verse displays God's promise reassuring Jacob's descent.

The witnesses agree the substance: God, the covenant God, bade Jacob fear not to go down, promising to make him a great nation there (Keil, Calvin, Gill).
